elements.sent-2411-568: In this section, we provide a detailed illustration of the Machine Cost Estimation Tool (MCET), which is implemented based on Algorithm~\ref{alg:ofpt .}
elements.sent-2411-569: The MCET is designed to estimate the computation cost of deploying machine learning models without the need for actual online deployment.
elements.sent-2411-570: This tool is particularly useful in scenarios where rapid iteration and cost-effective deployment are critical, such as in large-scale advertising systems.
elements.sent-2411-571: The core idea of MCET is to simulate the execution of models on a specified hardware configuration and measure their performance in terms of Queries Per Second (QPS) under a given response time limit.
elements.sent-2411-572: By leveraging these simulations, MCET can estimate the number of machines required to serve each model while meeting the desired performance constraints.
elements.sent-2411-573: This approach eliminates the need for costly and time-consuming online deployment, providing a reliable and efficient alternative for cost estimation.
elements.sent-2411-574: The algorithm begins by initializing a base model \( M_0 \) and simulating its execution on the specified hardware configuration \( \mathcal{MH \).}
elements.sent-2411-575: The QPS of the base model \( QPS_0 \) is measured under the response time limit \( T_{\text{limit \).}}
elements.sent-2411-576: Using this measurement, the total QPS capacity of the base model setup is calculated as \( QPS_{\text{total = req_0 \times QPS_0 \), where \( req_0 \) is the number of machines allocated to the base model.}}
elements.sent-2411-577: Subsequently, the algorithm iterates over the remaining models, loading their meta files and simulating their execution on the same hardware configuration.
elements.sent-2411-578: For each model, the QPS \( QPS_i \) is measured under the same response time limit, and the required number of machines \( req_i \) is calculated as \( req_i = \frac{QPS_{\text{total {QPS_i \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-579: The results are stored in a list and returned as the final output.
elements.sent-2411-580: The MCET tool, which implements this algorithm, allows us to estimate the machine requirements for tens of models within a short time frame.
elements.sent-2411-581: For instance, using a single machine with a single GPU, we can estimate the required number of machines for a single TensorFlow meta-file within half an hour.
elements.sent-2411-582: This capability significantly reduces the time and cost associated with model deployment and iteration.
elements.sent-2411-583: Furthermore, by combining the estimated machine requirements with the unit price of the specified hardware configuration, we can accurately calculate the expected machine cost.
elements.sent-2411-584: This approach is applicable to both training and serving cost estimation, with the algorithm being executed using the meta files specific to each process.
elements.sent-2411-585: For training cost estimation, labels are randomly constructed to simulate the training environment.
elements.sent-2411-586: In summary, MCET provides a standardized, reproducible, and efficient method for estimating computation costs, enabling rapid and cost-effective model deployment in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-587: Regrading Case Study 1: We set an upper limit on the model FLOPs to 143M and set the step size of the grid search as 16 and 128 for the feature embedding dimensions and the unit number of MLP layers, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-588: Additionally, we enforce that the number of units in any layer (except the output layer) is at least 1/20 of the number of units in the previous layer, as the reason discussed in appendix~\ref{sec:limitation_opt_mlp .}
elements.sent-2411-589: Regrading Case Study 2: We observe from the fitted data points that models in the 0.05B to 0.1B parameter range offer favorable cost-performance trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-590: Leveraging the scaling law for Transformer-based models, we therefore conduct a grid search within this interval with a step size of 0.01B.
elements.sent-2411-592: Given that the model size is moderate, we are able to use A10 GPUs for both training and serving.
elements.sent-2411-593: Notably, the scaling law we fit remains in terms of FLOPs and $R/R^*$.
elements.sent-2411-594: When the average number of tokens per query is known, there exists a straightforward relationship between model parameter count (in billions) and FLOPs.
elements.sent-2411-595: Regrading Case Study 3: As mentioned, we adopted an MLP model for the Pre-ranking stage and a DSSM model for the Matching stage.
elements.sent-2411-596: We used the machine cost estimation tool to additionally measure the machine costs of the DSSM model at different sizes.
elements.sent-2411-597: Similarly to the estimation of the MLP model, we solved the estimated machine costs of approximately 1000 different sizes of DSSM models based on the algorithm~\ref{alg:ofpt using 10 T4-GPU machines for about 2 days.}
elements.sent-2411-598: Regrading Case Study 4: For $Scene_1$ and $Scene_2$, we perform proportional scaling of the base models in both the Pre-ranking and Matching stages.
elements.sent-2411-599: The scaling ratio ranges from 0.1 to 3.0 with a step size of 0.1.
elements.sent-2411-600: Based on the grid search results and the overall ROI across stages, we ultimately decide to reduce the model size in $Scene_1$ by 10\% and increase the model size in $Scene_2$ by 50\%.
elements.sent-2411-601: Obtaining joint laws considering multiple factors, such as data, model size, and computational resources, is significantly more costly in industrial advertising settings than obtaining single-variable scaling laws.
elements.sent-2411-602: An especially promising direction for application is to model the joint law between model size and the computational quota allocation across stages in a cascade ranking system.
elements.sent-2411-603: Such a law could help answer critical questions: How should we rebalance ranking, pre-ranking, and matching stage budgets when scaling up the overall model? At what point does increasing model size in one stage yield diminishing returns due to bottlenecks in another? By formalizing these interactions, joint laws can deepen our understanding of cascade systems and guide principled, globally optimal configuration decisions with only a given budget.
elements.sent-2411-604: Although our optimization framework has achieved an overall 5.1\% improvement in ad revenue, a substantial portion of this gain arises not from breakthroughs in model architecture, but from correcting historically suboptimal resource allocation and coarse-grained manual tuning.
elements.sent-2411-605: Our results indicate that, unlike in NLP, simply scaling up computation alone is unlikely to bring similar levels of success to the advertising domain for models such as MLP, DSSM, or Transformer.
elements.sent-2411-606: Therefore, the next major leap in performance will likely require more disruptive innovations.
elements.sent-2411-607: One path is architectural rethinking—designing models with inherently better scaling efficiency, such as sparse, modular architectures~\citep{deepseek_moe_v2_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-608: Another is a data-centric paradigm shift: leveraging large language models or product-level innovations to improve the quality, semantic richness, and diversity of training signals.
elements.sent-2411-609: In this view, "scaling" must evolve beyond model size to include the scaling of data intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-610: Only through such foundational changes can we hope to achieve the kind of sustained, huge progress seen in NLP domains.
